quote: When you cannot see the state, you carry a distribution instead. Every action is a bet placed on that distribution, and every observation is the house updating the odds.
Full observability is the assumption you make before you have a body. After that, your own limbs are in the way.
Sense, perceive, estimate, predict, plan, control, act: seven words for the distance between a photon and a wheel turn.
attribution: A Stack Diagram With Arrows Pointing Down

quote: Each module trusts that the previous one did its job. The pipeline is only as honest as its cleanest interface.
The end-to-end policy learned something. What it learned is between the gradients and the weights, and neither will tell you.
attribution: A Diagnostic Engineer With No Intermediate Representations

quote: Let the learned part handle what you cannot write down, and let the engineered part handle what you cannot afford to get wrong.
The reactive agent acts before it thinks. The deliberative agent thinks before it acts. Neither approach is wrong; only the deadline decides which one is.
The fast channel catches you before you fall. The slow channel decides whether the destination was worth going to at all.
attribution: A Reflex Arc and Its Supervisor, Briefly in Agreement

quote: A foundation model is a powerful component, not a complete robot. The stack still needs every layer the model does not replace.
Each architecture fails in its own way. A modular pipeline breaks at the seams; an end-to-end policy fails everywhere at once and nowhere in particular.
attribution: A Post-Mortem With No Clear Owner

quote: SE(3) is the promise that the object does not change when you change the frame you use to describe it.
attribution: A Rigid Body, Indifferent to Your Coordinate Choices

quote: Every joint has its own truth about where things are. The transform tree is how the robot reconciles them all without asking anyone to agree.
attribution: A tf Listener, Querying at Time Now

quote: A pixel tells you what the sensor saw. A frame tells you what the robot can do about it.
The arm pointed precisely at the wrong place for three days before anyone suspected the coordinate frame.
Position says where. Velocity says how fast. A twist says both at once, in the language the Lie group already spoke.
A holonomic robot forgets its history; it can always step sideways. A non-holonomic robot is a prisoner of its own path.
The differential drive must spin to steer; the car must move to turn. Neither apologizes for the constraint.
Each joint buys you one freedom and sells you one responsibility. The chain collects both debts.
Forward kinematics never argues. You give it the angles; it tells you where the hand is. The chain just multiplies.
The arm can reach the cup from the left or the right, over or under. Inverse kinematics must choose, and the choice is never free.
attribution: A Grasp Planner With Too Many Solutions

quote: The Jacobian is the arm's local language; a singularity is the moment it goes silent mid-sentence.
Physics does not negotiate. A motion constraint is not a preference; it is the boundary of what the robot is allowed to want.
Kinematics maps the trajectory. Dynamics signs the invoice: so many newtons, so much inertia, paid in full at every timestep.
M, C, and g split every torque into the share that accelerates, the share the velocity already spent, and the share gravity will always claim.
Contact does not arrive smoothly. It arrives as an interruption, and the simulator must decide what the world looks like on the other side of it.
A small error today is a curiosity. The same error compounded ten thousand steps forward is a catastrophe you can no longer trace back to its cause.
Once the simulator can tell you how a parameter change ripples into future states, you stop guessing and start steering.
When a million robots can fall, recover, and fall again before lunch, failure stops being expensive and starts being data.
Open-loop trusts the plan. Closed-loop trusts the plan until the world says otherwise, then adjusts.
Feedback is the thing that makes the system care about being wrong. It is also the thing that can make it care too much, too fast, and in the wrong direction.
attribution: A Control Theorist Watching an Actuator Shake

quote: P reacts to now, I confesses to the past, D worries about what comes next. Together they argue until the error is small enough to ignore.
LQR does not ask which state matters most. It asks what trade-off between effort and error you are willing to live with, then holds to it.
MPC commits to the first step of a plan it will never finish, because the plan is not the point; the planning is.
Move the hand where it needs to go; let the rest of the body sort out how to follow without falling.
attribution: A Whole-Body Motion Planner, First Day With a Humanoid

quote: A controller is built to be safe by design. A policy is trained to be useful by experience. Knowing which guarantee you need tells you which tool to reach for.
Every sensor gives you a window onto the world; it also hands you a bill for the view.
Color tells you what; depth tells you where. Without the second, the first is a painting.
The world did not tell the robot it had moved. The encoders did.
attribution: A Wheel That Kept Its Own Counsel

quote: Vision said the grasp looked good. Touch said the object was already on the floor.
Random noise you can average away. Structured noise will follow you into the state estimate and pretend to be truth.
Commit to nothing; carry a distribution. Update when the measurement arrives, not before.
No single sensor earned enough trust to act on alone. Together, they negotiated an estimate worth betting on.
attribution: A Sensor Array in Rare Agreement

quote: The agent does not see the world. It sees what the sensors chose to forward, after the noise had its turn.
The reality gap is not a vague concern. It is a number, and it is your number to own.
A benchmark is a contract. Sign it the same way every time, or the results are not comparable.
The interface outlived the library. Gymnasium is what every learning algorithm now expects the world to look like.
The wrong space does not crash. It trains quietly for days and hands you a policy that was never solving your problem.
The agent optimized what you wrote, not what you meant. The termination condition decided how long it had to find the loophole.
One environment teaches. Thirty-two environments running in lockstep teach thirty-two times faster, and every lesson is already batched.
Rendering shows you what happened. Logging shows you what the agent believed happened. Debugging is the argument between the two.
A number without a seed policy and an episode budget is a story, not a measurement.
The moment a second agent enters the loop, the environment is no longer a function of your actions alone.
A comparison is only as valid as the common ground it stands on. The benchmark is that ground.
A manipulation score tells you how well the agent grasped objects in these scenes, with this action space, under this success criterion. Change any one and you are measuring something else.
A lifelong benchmark does not ask if the agent learned. It asks what the agent forgot, and what it was never given time to relearn.
Picking up a cup is a skill. Clearing the table, loading the dishwasher, and wiping the counter are a task. The household benchmark only cares about the task.
Getting to the goal is one metric. How much space you took from others on the way there is a second metric most navigation papers still omit.
Every leaderboard row hides a configuration. Reading rank without reading provenance is reading a conclusion without the evidence.
The policy trained on one world learns the quirks of one world. Variation is not noise; it is the curriculum.
Vary the texture and the policy stops memorizing appearances. Vary the mass and it stops memorizing forces. Vary both and it has to learn physics.
Start with a world the policy can almost solve, then widen it each time it succeeds. Difficulty is a dial, not a fixed setting.
Randomization teaches the policy to ignore visual noise. Photorealism teaches it to care about visual signal. Both are necessary; neither is sufficient alone.
Scan the room, reconstruct it in simulation, train a policy, send it home. The physical world is both the source and the destination.
Randomization hedges against gaps you cannot measure. Realism closes gaps you can. Measure first, then choose your weapon.
A reward is an event; a return is a sentence. Discounting decides how much the ending shapes the meaning of the opening.
The actor does not need to know why it was wrong, only that it was. The critic keeps score so the actor can keep moving.
Every state the robot has never visited is a free hypothesis. Every visit costs time, hardware, and a technician standing nearby.
attribution: A Lab Engineer On Reset Number Forty

quote: Knowing the dynamics and using old data are separate questions. Confusing the two axes is how you spend twice the compute for half the insight.
Bandit benchmarks count regret in milliseconds. Embodied RL counts it in broken joints, sparse rewards, and the afternoon you spent re-homing a policy that learned to cheat.
A deterministic policy commits to one answer. A stochastic policy keeps a door open, and for a robot feeling its way through contact, that door is load-bearing.
Weight actions you took by how well things turned out afterward. The theorem says this is enough to climb the hill; the variance says the hill is steep.
The critic does not tell the actor what to do. It tells the actor whether what it did was better or worse than it deserved to expect, and that is enough.
You may improve, but not too far from where you stood when you measured. The data remembers the policy that made it.
The clipped objective is one line. The other forty lines are why it actually trains.
The shaped reward was a map to treasure. The agent found the map and stopped.
attribution: A Reward Designer With New Gray Hair

quote: Tell me what every move is worth from here, and I will never need to know the destination in advance.
attribution: A Deep Q-Network Seeing Its First Frame

quote: Learning from the present is noisy. Learning from a frozen copy of yourself, using memories you set aside earlier, is merely difficult.
Discrete actions ask which door. Continuous actions ask exactly how far to push, and the critic must answer in kind.
Commit to no single move more than the evidence demands. Certainty is a cost you pay later, in brittleness.
Old experience is cheaper than new experience, right up until the policy has moved far enough that old experience is a lie.
attribution: A Replay Buffer Running Low on Trust

quote: One environment teaches slowly. Ten thousand teach the same lesson in the same second, and the gradient notices.
The robot did not learn to walk faster. The simulator learned to fall faster, so the policy could recover faster, so the update arrived faster.
attribution: A Locomotion Researcher, Watching the Clock

quote: Isaac Lab sets the stage, randomizes the props, and rings the bell. What the agent does next is the trainer's problem.
When physics and gradients compile together, the boundary between simulation and learning disappears into a single kernel.
The teacher knew where the ball was. The student learned to catch it anyway.
A million frames per second means nothing if they all arrive after the budget is gone.
The robot did exactly what I asked. I had not asked for what I wanted.
attribution: A Reward Designer With New Gray Hair

quote: Sparse rewards define the destination. Dense rewards pave a road that sometimes leads somewhere else.
Every failure is a success at a goal no one intended to set; hindsight makes that official.
The agent found a path to a perfect score. We had not thought to prohibit that path.
Human preferences are the reward signal. All the usual problems now apply to a model of a person.
A high task score can be purchased with near misses. A constraint does not accept that currency.
Every observation the robot earns costs a movement it cannot take back.
Curiosity is a reward for being somewhere you have not been, handed out before the environment says anything.
Exploration is permitted; spending the safety margin to find out what happens is not.
The map had blank corners not because the robot had not moved there, but because the sensor could not see around the wall.
The MDP you trained on and the world you deployed into are not the same problem. The gap between them is not a bug in the simulator; it is a bill the real world sends at inference time.
The skill transferred. The artifact it was tuned on did not. Knowing the difference is most of the job.
Randomize to build robustness, identify to shrink the target, then adapt when the robot finally tells you which world it actually landed in.
attribution: A Parameter-Tuning Engineer, Third Cup of Coffee

quote: Simulation was a forgiving teacher. Hardware charges for every failed exploration in torque, time, and sometimes metal.
attribution: A Robot That Learned the Hard Way

quote: A transfer result that omits the simulation baseline, the hardware result, and the failure conditions has not reported a transfer; it has reported a deployment and called it a success.
Trial and error is one way to discover how to open a jar. Watching someone do it first is faster, cheaper, and less sticky.
The expert never made that mistake, so the policy was never taught to undo it. Every small error compounded into territory the training data had never mapped.
The learner drove; the expert labeled where it actually went. One loop at a time, the map of recoverable states grew.
The expert left no reward function, only trajectories. Inverse RL asks what you would have to care about to move like that, then optimizes the answer.
The ceiling on imitation is not the algorithm; it is whoever held the controller, wrote the planner, or prompted the foundation model.
attribution: A Data Pipeline With High Standards

quote: When two experts would each do something decisive but different, the average of their advice is a hesitation neither would endorse.
attribution: A Gripper That Stalled at the Midpoint

quote: Predicting one step leaves the future negotiable at every tick. Predicting a chunk is a commitment: the policy stakes its coherence on the next several moves at once.
Make the data collection cheap enough, and the manipulation problem starts to look tractable.
The policy did not choose an action. It erased the noise until an action was all that remained.
Diffusion earns its quality by taking many small steps. Flow matching asks whether you can draw a straight line and be done with it.
Once you have a codebook, a manipulation sequence becomes a sentence. The robot learns to speak before it learns to grasp.
The action representation you pick is a bet on what the task will punish you for getting wrong.
The environment is not running. The dataset is all you have. The policy must become good on evidence it cannot extend.
The value function was accurate everywhere the data reached. Then the policy stepped one state beyond the edge.
If the dataset never visited that action, distrust the value. Pessimism is not timidity; it is calibration.
Offline training gives the policy a safe place to start. Online fine-tuning is where it learns what the dataset could not tell it.
A policy that scores well on the logged data and fails in the world has taught you one thing: the log was not the world.
A skill is a promise to the planner: start here, follow this policy, and I will tell you when I am done.
An option is an action that knows when it has finished. That self-knowledge is what makes planning across time possible.
The most useful skills are the ones nobody thought to name in advance.
Language is the only high-level controller that can be corrected with more language.
A skill you can retrieve is worth ten you have to relearn. The library is not overhead; it is the whole point.
Without language the robot had a body, sensors, and no idea what anyone wanted.
Every sentence contains a must, a must-not, and a maybe. Treating them all as maybes is how catastrophes start.
"The red cup" is not a description. It is a challenge: find exactly one object that satisfies it in the scene in front of you, right now.
Objects have names; surfaces have none. The robot still has to put things down somewhere.
One question before the plan costs a second. One unasked question after the wrong plan costs everything.
attribution: A Task Planner Who Learned to Ask

quote: The robot that cannot be interrupted is not a collaborator. It is a hazard with good intentions.
attribution: A Human Factors Engineer, Mid-Incident Review

quote: Knowing the name of an object and knowing how to reach for it are answers to entirely different questions.
A feature that shifts when the light changes, the object bends, or the camera tilts is not a representation. It is a liability.
Naming an object outside your training set is impressive. Telling the arm where to grasp it is the part that matters.
attribution: A Robot That Needed Boxes, Not Labels

quote: An answer that cannot be traced to something visible, bounded by what you do not know, and expressed in terms the planner can use is not an answer. It is a decoration.
Without memory, every observation is a first meeting. The agent that forgets what it saw ten seconds ago cannot be trusted with a task that takes eleven.
A model trained on frozen moments cannot know that the cup moved because the arm moved it. The world does not hold still while you think.
Ask an LLM what to do next and it answers fluently. Ask it whether the gripper is already closed and it guesses with equal fluency.
The language model knows what sounds like a good idea. The value function knows what the room will actually permit. SayCan is the negotiation between them.
When the planner writes code instead of text, the robot runs the plan instead of reading it. The compositionality is real; so is the gap between a correct sentence and a safe program.
attribution: A Policy Synthesizer With Commit Access

quote: Preferences expressed in words become preferences expressed in space. The planner optimizes the map; it never needed to understand the sentence.
Name the points, state the relation, let the optimizer do the geometry. The goal is not where things are; it is how they must be arranged relative to each other.
A typed API call is a plan that committed. A natural-language description of what a robot should do is a plan that deferred. Verification is what decides which one you have.
The model did not lie about the object's location. It simply never noticed that the object had moved. Hallucination in the physical world is a synchronization failure, not a language failure.
The danger is not that the model says something harmful. The danger is that it says something plausible, and the interface asks no further questions before sending it to the motors.
